comments.body: "If not, what are some other ways I can accomplish this?" Just reverse the list.
This was a doh! moment for sure. Just reverse the list.. beautiful!

answers.body:
You could make the list descending by reversing it:

Collection<?> collection = ...;
Collections.reverse(collection);

PS: why is your class named States? From what I see the class represents a State...

SequencedCollection

In Java 21+, define your edges as a SequencedCollection, a new super-interface of ArrayList, LinkedList, LinkedHashSet, TreeSet, and more. (See JEP 431.)

As the implementation of SequencedCollection, choose a class that automatically sorts objects as you add them. Here we choose TreeSet as such a class.

Comparable & Comparator

But how will the TreeSet know how to sort objects of type State? We need to declare that our State class implements Comparable. Then we implement the single required method compareTo. Our implementation of that method leverages the handy Comparator interface with its static method comparing.

And our State class needs to override Object#toString to print the name of the state.

package work.basil.example.collections;

import org.jspecify.annotations.NonNull;

import java.util.Comparator;
import java.util.SequencedCollection;
import java.util.TreeSet;

public class State implements Comparable < State >
{
    private static final Comparator < State > comparator = Comparator.comparing( State :: name );

    final String name;
    final SequencedCollection < State > edges = new TreeSet <>();

    public State ( final String theName )
    {
        this.name = theName;
    }

    public String name ( ) { return this.name; }

    // Implement `Comparable`.
    @Override
    public int compareTo ( @NonNull final State other )
    {
        return State.comparator.compare( this , other );
    }

    // Override `Object#toString`.
    @Override
    public String toString ( )
    {
        return "State{" +
                "name='" + name + '\'' +
                '}';
    }
}

Example usage follows. Notice the call to SequencedCollection#reversed which seturns a reverse-ordered view of the collection.

State wa = new State( "Washington" );
State id = new State( "Idaho" );
State or = new State( "Oregon" );
State ca = new State( "California" );

wa.edges.addAll( List.of( or , id ) ); // `TreeSet` automatically sorts the added objects.

System.out.println( "Edges sorted: " + wa.edges );
System.out.println( "Edges reversed: " + wa.edges.reversed() );

Edges sorted: [State{name='Idaho'}, State{name='Oregon'}]

Edges reversed: [State{name='Oregon'}, State{name='Idaho'}]

title: Design a compareTo that can sort in ascending or descending order
body:
I have a class States that is an object that contains a name, and a list of states that we consider edges. I would like to sort this list of edges by name, in both ascending and descending order. I had an idea of overriding the compareTo function and leveraging Collections.sort(). 

public class State { 
    private String name;
    private ArrayList<States> edges;      
    ...

    public ArrayList<States> getEdges(){
        return edges;
    }

    public int compareTo(State s, int type){
        switch(type){
            case 0: 
                // we want ascending order, return as normal
                return this.name.compareTo(s.getName());
            case 1:
                // we want descending order, negate our answer
                return -this.name.compareTo(s.getName());
        }
        return -1;
     }

Is it possible to use Collection.sort(), but be able to provide the 'type' parameter to specify whether I want ascending or descending order? If not, what are some other ways I can accomplish this?
